summary:
The first modern Olympic Games, held in Athens, Greece in April 1896, revived the ancient Greek athletic tradition after a gap of some 1,500 years and established the international sporting competition that has grown into one of the world's most significant periodic events. The Athens Games were the realization of the vision of French educator and internationalist Pierre de Coubertin, who had championed the revival of the Olympics as a means of promoting international understanding, physical culture, and the amateur athletic ideal.

De Coubertin convened the International Olympic Congress in Paris in 1894, which agreed to revive the Games and chose Athens as the site for the inaugural modern Olympics in 1896 — a choice that honored the Games' classical origins and gave Greece the honor of hosting the revival. The Greek government was initially reluctant due to financial difficulties, but the enthusiasm of Crown Prince Constantine and the financial support of wealthy Greek expatriate Georgios Averoff, who funded the restoration of the ancient Panathinaiko Stadium, made the Games possible.

Approximately 241 athletes from 14 nations competed in 43 events across 9 sports: athletics (track and field), cycling, fencing, gymnastics, shooting, swimming, tennis, weightlifting, and wrestling. The United States won the most gold medals (11), with Greece second. American James Connolly became the first Olympic champion in over 1,500 years when he won the triple jump on the first day of competition. The marathon — a new event designed to honor the legendary run from Marathon to Athens — was won by Greek runner Spyridon Louis, to enormous popular celebration.

The Athens Games were a modest event by later standards but an organizational success that established the template for the modern Olympics. The quadrennial cycle, the international participation, the opening and closing ceremonies, and the amateur ideal of sport for its own sake became defining features. The Games have grown continuously since, becoming intertwined with national prestige, commercial interests, and political controversies that de Coubertin could not have imagined.
